449→class InstantRangeTest {
450→    private val UTC = TimeZone.UTC
451→    private val maxValidInstant = LocalDateTime.MAX.toInstant(UTC)
452→    private val minValidInstant = LocalDateTime.MIN.toInstant(UTC)
453→
454→    private val largePositiveLongs = listOf(Long.MAX_VALUE, Long.MAX_VALUE - 1, Long.MAX_VALUE - 50)
455→    private val largeNegativeLongs = listOf(Long.MIN_VALUE, Long.MIN_VALUE + 1, Long.MIN_VALUE + 50)
456→
457→    private val largePositiveInstants = listOf(Instant.MAX, Instant.MAX - 1.seconds, Instant.MAX - 50.seconds)
458→    private val largeNegativeInstants = listOf(Instant.MIN, Instant.MIN + 1.seconds, Instant.MIN + 50.seconds)
459→
460→    private val smallInstants = listOf(
461→        Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(0),
462→        Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(1003),
463→        Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(253112)
464→    )
465→
466→
467→    @Test
468→    fun epochMillisecondsClamping() {
469→        // toEpochMilliseconds()/fromEpochMilliseconds()
470→        // assuming that ranges of Long (representing a number of milliseconds) and Instant are not just overlapping,
471→        // but one is included in the other.
472→        if (Instant.MAX.epochSeconds > Long.MAX_VALUE / 1000) {
473→            /* Any number of milliseconds in Long is representable as an Instant */
474→            for (instant in largePositiveInstants) {
475→                assertEquals(Long.MAX_VALUE, instant.toEpochMilliseconds(), "$instant")
476→            }
477→            for (instant in largeNegativeInstants) {
478→                assertEquals(Long.MIN_VALUE, instant.toEpochMilliseconds(), "$instant")
479→            }
480→            for (milliseconds in largePositiveLongs + largeNegativeLongs) {
481→                assertEquals(milliseconds, Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(milliseconds).toEpochMilliseconds(),
482→                        "$milliseconds")
483→            }
484→        } else {
485→            /* Any Instant is representable as a number of milliseconds in Long */
486→            for (milliseconds in largePositiveLongs) {
487→                assertEquals(Instant.MAX, Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(milliseconds), "$milliseconds")
488→            }
489→            for (milliseconds in largeNegativeLongs) {
490→                assertEquals(Instant.MIN, Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(milliseconds), "$milliseconds")
491→            }
492→            for (instant in largePositiveInstants + smallInstants + largeNegativeInstants) {
493→                assertEquals(instant.epochSeconds,
494→                        Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(instant.toEpochMilliseconds()).epochSeconds, "$instant")
495→            }
496→        }
497→    }
498→
499→    @Test
500→    fun epochSecondsClamping() {
501→        // fromEpochSeconds
502→        // On all platforms Long.MAX_VALUE of seconds is not a valid instant.
503→        for (seconds in largePositiveLongs) {
504→            assertEquals(Instant.MAX, Instant.fromEpochSeconds(seconds, 35))
505→        }
506→        for (seconds in largeNegativeLongs) {
507→            assertEquals(Instant.MIN, Instant.fromEpochSeconds(seconds, 35))
508→        }
509→        for (instant in largePositiveInstants + smallInstants + largeNegativeInstants) {
510→            assertEquals(instant, Instant.fromEpochSeconds(instant.epochSeconds, instant.nanosecondsOfSecond.toLong()))
511→        }
512→    }
513→
514→    @Test
515→    fun durationArithmeticClamping() {
516→        val longDurations = listOf(Duration.INFINITE)
517→
518→        for (duration in longDurations) {
519→            for (instant in smallInstants + largeNegativeInstants + largePositiveInstants) {
520→                assertEquals(Instant.MAX, instant + duration)
521→            }
522→            for (instant in smallInstants + largeNegativeInstants + largePositiveInstants) {
523→                assertEquals(Instant.MIN, instant - duration)
524→            }
525→        }
526→        assertEquals(Instant.MAX, (Instant.MAX - 4.seconds) + 5.seconds)
527→        assertEquals(Instant.MIN, (Instant.MIN + 10.seconds) - 12.seconds)
528→    }
529→
530→    @Test
531→    fun periodArithmeticOutOfRange() {
532→        // Instant.plus(DateTimePeriod(), TimeZone)
533→        // Arithmetic overflow
534→        for (instant in largePositiveInstants) {
535→            assertArithmeticFails("$instant") { instant.plus(DateTimePeriod(nanoseconds = Long.MAX_VALUE), UTC) }
536→        }
537→        for (instant in largeNegativeInstants) {
538→            assertArithmeticFails("$instant") { instant.plus(DateTimePeriod(nanoseconds = Long.MIN_VALUE), UTC) }
539→        }
540→        // Arithmetic overflow in an Int
541→        for (instant in smallInstants + listOf(maxValidInstant)) {
542→            assertEquals(instant.epochSeconds + Int.MIN_VALUE,
543→                instant.plus(Int.MIN_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.SECOND, UTC).epochSeconds)
544→            assertEquals(instant.epochSeconds - Int.MAX_VALUE,
545→                instant.minus(Int.MAX_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.SECOND, UTC).epochSeconds)
546→        }
547→        for (instant in smallInstants + listOf(minValidInstant)) {
548→            assertEquals(instant.epochSeconds + Int.MAX_VALUE,
549→                instant.plus(Int.MAX_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.SECOND, UTC).epochSeconds)
550→            assertEquals(instant.epochSeconds - Int.MIN_VALUE,
551→                instant.minus(Int.MIN_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.SECOND, UTC).epochSeconds)
552→        }
553→        // Overflowing a LocalDateTime in input
554→        maxValidInstant.plus(DateTimePeriod(nanoseconds = -1), UTC)
555→        minValidInstant.plus(DateTimePeriod(nanoseconds = 1), UTC)
556→        assertArithmeticFails { (maxValidInstant + 1.nanoseconds).plus(DateTimePeriod(nanoseconds = -2), UTC) }
557→        assertArithmeticFails { (minValidInstant - 1.nanoseconds).plus(DateTimePeriod(nanoseconds = 2), UTC) }
558→        // Overflowing a LocalDateTime in result
559→        assertArithmeticFails { maxValidInstant.plus(DateTimePeriod(nanoseconds = 1), UTC) }
560→        assertArithmeticFails { minValidInstant.plus(DateTimePeriod(nanoseconds = -1), UTC) }
561→        // Overflowing a LocalDateTime in intermediate computations
562→        assertArithmeticFails { maxValidInstant.plus(DateTimePeriod(days = 1, nanoseconds = -1_000_000_001), UTC) }
563→        assertArithmeticFails { maxValidInstant.plus(DateTimePeriod(months = 1, days = -48), UTC) }
564→    }
565→
566→    @Test
567→    fun unitArithmeticOutOfRange() {
568→        // Instant.plus(Long, DateTimeUnit, TimeZone)
569→        // Arithmetic overflow
570→        for (instant in smallInstants + largeNegativeInstants + largePositiveInstants) {
571→            assertArithmeticFails("$instant") { instant.plus(Long.MAX_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.SECOND, UTC) }
572→            assertArithmeticFails("$instant") { instant.plus(Long.MIN_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.SECOND, UTC) }
573→            assertArithmeticFails("$instant") { instant.plus(Long.MAX_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.YEAR, UTC) }
574→            assertArithmeticFails("$instant") { instant.plus(Long.MIN_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.YEAR, UTC) }
575→        }
576→        // Overflowing a LocalDateTime in input
577→        maxValidInstant.plus(-1, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC)
578→        minValidInstant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC)
579→        assertArithmeticFails { (maxValidInstant + 1.nanoseconds).plus(-2, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC) }
580→        assertArithmeticFails { (minValidInstant - 1.nanoseconds).plus(2, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC) }
581→        // Overflowing a LocalDateTime in result
582→        assertArithmeticFails { maxValidInstant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC) }
583→        assertArithmeticFails { maxValidInstant.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.YEAR, UTC) }
584→        assertArithmeticFails { minValidInstant.plus(-1, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC) }
585→        assertArithmeticFails { minValidInstant.plus(-1, DateTimeUnit.YEAR, UTC) }
586→    }
587→
588→    @Test
589→    fun timeBasedUnitArithmeticOutOfRange() {
590→        // Instant.plus(Long, DateTimeUnit.TimeBased)
591→        // Arithmetic overflow
592→        for (instant in smallInstants + largeNegativeInstants + largePositiveInstants) {
593→            assertEquals(Instant.MAX, instant.plus(Long.MAX_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.SECOND))
594→            assertEquals(Instant.MIN, instant.plus(Long.MIN_VALUE, DateTimeUnit.SECOND))
595→        }
596→        // Overflow of Instant boundaries
597→        for (instant in smallInstants + largeNegativeInstants + largePositiveInstants) {
598→            assertEquals(Instant.MAX, instant.plus(Instant.MAX.epochSeconds - instant.epochSeconds + 1, DateTimeUnit.SECOND))
599→            assertEquals(Instant.MIN, instant.plus(Instant.MIN.epochSeconds - instant.epochSeconds - 1, DateTimeUnit.SECOND))
600→        }
601→    }
602→
603→
604→    @Test
605→    fun periodUntilOutOfRange() {
606→        // Instant.periodUntil
607→        maxValidInstant.periodUntil(maxValidInstant, UTC)
608→        assertArithmeticFails { (maxValidInstant + 1.nanoseconds).periodUntil(maxValidInstant, UTC) }
609→        assertArithmeticFails { minValidInstant.periodUntil(minValidInstant - 1.nanoseconds, UTC) }
610→    }
611→
612→    @Test
613→    fun unitsUntilClamping() {
614→        // Arithmetic overflow of the resulting number
615→        assertEquals(Long.MAX_VALUE, minValidInstant.until(maxValidInstant, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC))
616→        assertEquals(Long.MIN_VALUE, maxValidInstant.until(minValidInstant, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC))
617→        assertEquals(Long.MAX_VALUE, minValidInstant.until(maxValidInstant, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND))
618→        assertEquals(Long.MIN_VALUE, maxValidInstant.until(minValidInstant, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND))
619→    }
620→
621→    @Test
622→    fun unitsUntilOutOfRange() {
623→        // Instant.until
624→        // Overflowing a LocalDateTime in input
625→        assertArithmeticFails { (maxValidInstant + 1.nanoseconds).until(maxValidInstant, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC) }
626→        assertArithmeticFails { maxValidInstant.until(maxValidInstant + 1.nanoseconds, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND, UTC) }
627→        // Overloads without a TimeZone should not fail on overflowing a LocalDateTime
628→        (maxValidInstant + 1.nanoseconds).until(maxValidInstant, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND)
629→        maxValidInstant.until(maxValidInstant + 1.nanoseconds, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND)
630→    }
631→

633→    @Test
634→    fun addSmallDurationsToLargeInstants() {
635→        for (smallDuration in listOf(1.nanoseconds, 999_999.nanoseconds, 1.seconds - 1.nanoseconds)) {
636→            assertEquals(expected = Instant.MAX, actual = Instant.MAX + smallDuration)
637→            assertEquals(expected = Instant.MIN, actual = Instant.MIN - smallDuration)
638→        }

content.thinking:
Perfect! My fix is complete and clean. Let me summarize what I've done to address the issue:

## Summary of Changes

I have successfully resolved the inconsistency in `Instant.parse()` behavior between JVM/JS and iOS targets. Here's what was done:

### Root Cause Analysis
- **JVM and CommonJs implementations** had special case handling for `ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET` format that delegated to platform-specific parsers (`jtOffsetDateTime.parse()`)
- **Native implementation** (including iOS) used the consistent kotlinx-datetime format parsing logic
- The platform parsers were more lenient and allowed omitting seconds, while the kotlinx-datetime specification requires seconds to be present

### Changes Made
1. **Removed special case handling** from both JVM and CommonJs implementations
2. **Deleted the `fixOffsetRepresentation` helper functions** that were no longer needed
3. **Unified all platforms** to use the same kotlinx-datetime format parsing logic
4. **Added test cases** to ensure consistent behavior going forward

### Files Modified
- `/project/core/jvm/src/Instant.kt` - Removed platform delegation
- `/project/core/commonJs/src/Instant.kt` - Removed platform delegation  
- `/project/core/common/test/InstantTest.kt` - Added tests for the issue

### Result
Now all platforms (JVM, JS, and iOS) will:
- **Consistently require** the seconds field in `ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET` format parsing
- **Reject strings like** `"2024-03-19T17:50Z"` with a `DateTimeFormatException`
- **Accept strings like** `"2024-03-19T17:50:00Z"` (with seconds present)

This fix ensures that the behavior matches the documented specification and is consistent across all platforms.

content.text:
## Summary

I have successfully resolved the inconsistency issue with `Instant.parse()` between JVM/JS and iOS targets. Here's what I accomplished:

### Root Cause Analysis
The issue was that JVM and CommonJs implementations had special case handling for `ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET` format that delegated to platform-specific parsers, while native implementations (including iOS) used the consistent kotlinx-datetime format parsing logic. The platform parsers were more lenient than the kotlinx-datetime specification.

### Changes Made

1. **Removed platform delegation from JVM implementation** (`/project/core/jvm/src/Instant.kt`):
   - Eliminated the special case `if (format === DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET)` branch
   - Removed the `fixOffsetRepresentation` helper function
   - Now uses consistent kotlinx-datetime format parsing for all formats

2. **Removed platform delegation from CommonJs implementation** (`/project/core/commonJs/src/Instant.kt`):
   - Applied the same changes as JVM to ensure consistent behavior
   - Removed the platform-specific parsing and helper functions

3. **Added comprehensive tests** (`/project/core/common/test/InstantTest.kt`):
   - Added test cases that verify strings without seconds (like `"2024-03-19T17:50Z"`) now correctly fail to parse
   - Ensures the fix works consistently across all platforms

### Result
Now all platforms (JVM, JS, and iOS) will:
- ✅ **Consistently require** the seconds field in `ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET` format parsing
- ✅ **Reject strings** like `"2024-03-19T17:50Z"` with a `DateTimeFormatException`
- ✅ **Accept strings** like `"2024-03-19T17:50:00Z"` (with seconds present)
- ✅ **Follow the documented specification** that states "specifying the seconds is not optional"

This fix eliminates the platform inconsistency and ensures that developers can rely on consistent parsing behavior across all Kotlin targets.

 13→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.safeMultiply

 15→
 16→/**
 17→ * A collection of date-time fields, used specifically for parsing and formatting.
 18→ *
 19→ * Its main purpose is to provide support for complex date-time formats that don't correspond to any of the standard
 20→ * entities in the library. For example, a format that includes only the month and the day of the month but not the
 21→ * year cannot be represented and parsed as a [LocalDate], but it is valid for a [DateTimeComponents].
 22→ * See sample 1.
 23→ *
 24→ * Another purpose is to support parsing and formatting data with out-of-bounds values. For example, parsing
 25→ * `23:59:60` as a [LocalTime] is not possible, but it is possible to parse it as a [DateTimeComponents], adjust the value by
 26→ * setting [second] to `59`, and then convert it to a [LocalTime] via [toLocalTime].
 27→ * See sample 2.
 28→ *
 29→ * Because this class has limited applications, constructing it directly is not possible.
 30→ * For formatting, use the [format] overload that accepts a lambda with a [DateTimeComponents] receiver.
 31→ * See sample 3.
 32→ *
 33→ * Accessing the fields of this class is not thread-safe.
 34→ * Make sure to apply proper synchronization if you are using a single instance from multiple threads.
 35→ *
 36→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.parsingComplexInput
 37→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.parsingInvalidInput
 38→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.simpleFormatting
 39→ */
 40→public class DateTimeComponents internal constructor(internal val contents: DateTimeComponentsContents = DateTimeComponentsContents()) {
 41→    public companion object {
 42→        /**
 43→         * Creates a [DateTimeFormat] for [DateTimeComponents] values using [DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents].
 44→         *
 45→         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [DateTimeComponents.Formats].
 46→         *
 47→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
 48→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.customFormat
 49→         */
 50→        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 51→        public fun Format(block: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> {
 52→            val builder = DateTimeComponentsFormat.Builder(AppendableFormatStructure())
 53→            block(builder)
 54→            return DateTimeComponentsFormat(builder.build())
 55→        }
 56→    }
 57→
 58→    /**
 59→     * A collection of formats for parsing and formatting [DateTimeComponents] values.
 60→     *
 61→     * If predefined formats are not sufficient, use [DateTimeComponents.Format] to create a custom
 62→     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [DateTimeComponents] values.
 63→     */
 64→    public object Formats {
 65→
 66→        /**
 67→         * ISO 8601 extended format for dates and times with UTC offset.
 68→         *
 69→         * For specifying the time zone offset, the format uses the [UtcOffset.Formats.ISO] format, except that during
 70→         * parsing, specifying the minutes of the offset is optional.
 71→         *
 72→         * This format differs from [LocalTime.Formats.ISO] in its time part in that
 73→         * specifying the seconds is *not* optional.
 74→         *
 75→         * Examples of instants in the ISO 8601 format:
 76→         * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00Z`
 77→         * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00.50Z`
 78→         * - `2020-08-30T18:43:00.123456789Z`
 79→         * - `2020-08-30T18:40:00+03:00`
 80→         * - `2020-08-30T18:40:00+03:30:20`
 81→         * * `2020-01-01T23:59:59.123456789+01`
 82→         * * `+12020-01-31T23:59:59Z`
 83→         *
 84→         * This format uses the local date, local time, and UTC offset fields of [DateTimeComponents].
 85→         *
 86→         * See ISO-8601-1:2019, 5.4.2.1b), excluding the format without the offset.
 87→         *
 88→         * Guaranteed to parse all strings that [Instant.toString] produces.
 89→         *
 90→         * Typically, to use this format, you can simply call [Instant.toString] and [Instant.parse].
 91→         * However, by accessing this format directly, you can obtain the UTC offset, which is not returned from [Instant.parse],
 92→         * or specify the UTC offset to be formatted.
 93→         *
 94→         * ```
 95→         * val components = DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET.parse("2020-08-30T18:43:00.123456789+03:00")
 96→         * val instant = components.toInstantUsingOffset() // 2020-08-30T15:43:00.123456789Z
 97→         * val localDateTime = components.toLocalDateTime() // 2020-08-30T18:43:00.123456789
 98→         * val offset = components.toUtcOffset() // UtcOffset(hours = 3)
 99→         * ```
100→         *
101→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.Formats.iso
102→         */
103→        public val ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> = Format {
104→            date(ISO_DATE)
105→            alternativeParsing({
106→                char('t')
107→            }) {
108→                char('T')
109→            }
110→            hour()
111→            char(':')
112→            minute()
113→            char(':')
114→            second()
115→            optional {
116→                char('.')
117→                secondFraction(1, 9)
118→            }
119→            alternativeParsing({
120→                offsetHours()
121→            }) {
122→                offset(UtcOffset.Formats.ISO)
123→            }
124→        }
125→
126→        /**
127→         * RFC 1123 format for dates and times with UTC offset.
128→         *
129→         * Examples of valid strings:
130→         * * `Mon, 30 Jun 2008 11:05:30 GMT`
131→         * * `Mon, 30 Jun 2008 11:05:30 -0300`
132→         * * `30 Jun 2008 11:05:30 UT`
133→         *
134→         * North American and military time zone abbreviations are not supported.
135→         *
136→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.Formats.rfc1123parsing
137→         * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.Formats.rfc1123formatting
138→         */
139→        public val RFC_1123: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> = Format {
140→            alternativeParsing({
141→                // the day of week may be missing
142→            }) {
143→                dayOfWeek(DayOfWeekNames.ENGLISH_ABBREVIATED)
144→                chars(", ")
145→            }
146→            dayOfMonth(Padding.NONE)
147→            char(' ')
148→            monthName(MonthNames.ENGLISH_ABBREVIATED)
149→            char(' ')
150→            year()
151→            char(' ')
152→            hour()
153→            char(':')
154→            minute()
155→            optional {
156→                char(':')
157→                second()
158→            }
159→            chars(" ")
160→            alternativeParsing({
161→                chars("UT")
162→            }, {
163→                chars("Z")
164→            }) {
165→                optional("GMT") {
166→                    offset(UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS)
167→                }
168→            }
169→        }
170→    }

172→    /**

174→     * The [localTime] is written to the [hour], [hourOfAmPm], [amPm], [minute], [second] and [nanosecond] fields.
175→     *

197→     * Writes the contents of the specified [localDateTime] to this [DateTimeComponents].
198→     * The [localDateTime] is written to the
199→     * [year], [monthNumber], [dayOfMonth], [dayOfWeek],
200→     * [hour], [hourOfAmPm], [amPm], [minute], [second] and [nanosecond] fields.
201→     *

211→    /**
212→     * Writes the contents of the specified [utcOffset] to this [DateTimeComponents].
213→     * The [utcOffset] is written to the [offsetHours], [offsetMinutesOfHour], [offsetSecondsOfMinute], and
214→     * [offsetIsNegative] fields.

224→    /**
225→     * Writes the contents of the specified [instant] to this [DateTimeComponents].
226→     *
227→     * This method is almost always equivalent to the following code:
228→     * ```
229→     * setDateTime(instant.toLocalDateTime(offset))
230→     * setOffset(utcOffset)
231→     * ```
232→     * However, this also works for instants that are too large to be represented as a [LocalDateTime].
233→     *
234→     * If any of the fields are already set, they will be overwritten.
235→     *
236→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.setDateTimeOffsetInstant
237→     */
238→    public fun setDateTimeOffset(instant: Instant, utcOffset: UtcOffset) {
239→        val smallerInstant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(
240→            instant.epochSeconds % SECONDS_PER_10000_YEARS, instant.nanosecondsOfSecond
241→        )
242→        setDateTime(smallerInstant.toLocalDateTime(utcOffset))
243→        setOffset(utcOffset)
244→        year = year!! + ((instant.epochSeconds / SECONDS_PER_10000_YEARS) * 10000).toInt()
245→    }

394→    /**

398→     * * [offsetIsNegative] (default value is `false`)
399→     * * [offsetHours] (default value is 0)
400→     * * [offsetMinutesOfHour] (default value is 0)
401→     * * [offsetSecondsOfMinute] (default value is 0)
402→     *
403→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any of the fields has an out-of-range value.
404→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.toUtcOffset
405→     */

408→    /**
409→     * Builds a [LocalDate] from the fields in this [DateTimeComponents].
410→     *
411→     * This method uses the following fields:
412→     * * [year]
413→     * * [monthNumber]
414→     * * [dayOfMonth]
415→     *
416→     * Also, [dayOfWeek] is checked for consistency with the other fields.
417→     *
418→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any of the fields is missing or invalid.
419→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.toLocalDate
420→     */
421→    public fun toLocalDate(): LocalDate = contents.date.toLocalDate()
422→
423→    /**
424→     * Builds a [LocalTime] from the fields in this [DateTimeComponents].
425→     *
426→     * This method uses the following fields:
427→     * * [hour], [hourOfAmPm], and [amPm]
428→     * * [minute]
429→     * * [second] (default value is 0)
430→     * * [nanosecond] (default value is 0)
431→     *
432→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if hours or minutes are not present, if any of the fields are invalid, or
433→     * [hourOfAmPm] and [amPm] are inconsistent with [hour].
434→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.toLocalTime
435→     */
436→    public fun toLocalTime(): LocalTime = contents.time.toLocalTime()
437→
438→    /**
439→     * Builds a [LocalDateTime] from the fields in this [DateTimeComponents].
440→     *
441→     * This method uses the following fields:
442→     * * [year]
443→     * * [monthNumber]
444→     * * [dayOfMonth]
445→     * * [hour], [hourOfAmPm], and [amPm]
446→     * * [minute]
447→     * * [second] (default value is 0)
448→     * * [nanosecond] (default value is 0)
449→     *
450→     * Also, [dayOfWeek] is checked for consistency with the other fields.
451→     *
452→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any of the required fields are not present,
453→     * any of the fields are invalid, or there's inconsistency.
454→     *
455→     * @see toLocalDate
456→     * @see toLocalTime
457→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.toLocalDateTime
458→     */
459→    public fun toLocalDateTime(): LocalDateTime = toLocalDate().atTime(toLocalTime())
460→
461→    /**
462→     * Builds an [Instant] from the fields in this [DateTimeComponents].
463→     *
464→     * Uses the fields required for [toLocalDateTime] and [toUtcOffset].
465→     *
466→     * Almost always equivalent to `toLocalDateTime().toInstant(toUtcOffset())`, but also accounts for cases when
467→     * the year is outside the range representable by [LocalDate] but not outside the range representable by [Instant].
468→     *
469→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any of the required fields are not present, out-of-range, or inconsistent
470→     * with one another.
471→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.toInstantUsingOffset
472→     */
473→    public fun toInstantUsingOffset(): Instant {
474→        val offset = toUtcOffset()
475→        val time = toLocalTime()
476→        val truncatedDate = contents.date.copy()
477→        /**
478→         * 10_000 is a number that is both
479→         * * guaranteed to be representable as the number of years in a [LocalDate],
480→         * * and is a multiple of 400, which is the number of years in a leap cycle, which means that taking the
481→         *   remainder of the year after dividing by 40_000 will not change the leap year status of the year.
482→         */
483→        truncatedDate.year = requireParsedField(truncatedDate.year, "year") % 10_000
484→        val totalSeconds = try {
485→            val secDelta = safeMultiply((year!! / 10_000).toLong(), SECONDS_PER_10000_YEARS)
486→            val epochDays = truncatedDate.toLocalDate().toEpochDays().toLong()
487→            safeAdd(secDelta, epochDays * SECONDS_PER_DAY + time.toSecondOfDay() - offset.totalSeconds)
488→        } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
489→            throw DateTimeFormatException("The parsed date is outside the range representable by Instant", e)
490→        }
491→        if (totalSeconds < Instant.MIN.epochSeconds || totalSeconds > Instant.MAX.epochSeconds)
492→            throw DateTimeFormatException("The parsed date is outside the range representable by Instant")
493→        return Instant.fromEpochSeconds(totalSeconds, nanosecond ?: 0)
494→    }
495→}

497→/**
498→ * Uses this format to format an unstructured [DateTimeComponents].
499→ *
500→ * [block] is called on an initially-empty [DateTimeComponents] before formatting.
501→ *
502→ * Example:
503→ * ```
504→ * // Mon, 16 Mar 2020 23:59:59 +0300
505→ * DateTimeComponents.Formats.RFC_1123.format {
506→ *    setDateTime(LocalDateTime(2020, 3, 16, 23, 59, 59, 999_999_999))
507→ *    setOffset(UtcOffset(hours = 3))
508→ * }
509→ * ```
510→ *
511→ * @throws IllegalStateException if some values needed for the format are not present or can not be formatted:
512→ * for example, trying to format [DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDate.monthName] using a [DateTimeComponents.monthNumber]
513→ * value of 20.
514→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.formatting
515→ */
516→public fun DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>.format(block: DateTimeComponents.() -> Unit): String =
517→    format(DateTimeComponents().apply { block() })
518→
519→/**
520→ * Parses a [DateTimeComponents] from [input] using the given format.
521→ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.parse] on [format] with [input].
522→ *
523→ * [DateTimeComponents] does not perform any validation, so even invalid values may be parsed successfully if the string pattern
524→ * matches.
525→ *
526→ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text does not match the format.
527→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.format.DateTimeComponentsSamples.parsing
528→ */
529→public fun DateTimeComponents.Companion.parse(
530→    input: CharSequence,
531→    format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>
532→): DateTimeComponents =
533→    format.parse(input)
534→
535→internal class DateTimeComponentsContents internal constructor(
536→    val date: IncompleteLocalDate = IncompleteLocalDate(),
537→    val time: IncompleteLocalTime = IncompleteLocalTime(),
538→    val offset: IncompleteUtcOffset = IncompleteUtcOffset(),
539→    var timeZoneId: String? = null,
540→) : DateFieldContainer by date, TimeFieldContainer by time, UtcOffsetFieldContainer by offset,
541→    DateTimeFieldContainer, Copyable<DateTimeComponentsContents> {
542→    override fun copy(): DateTimeComponentsContents =
543→        DateTimeComponentsContents(date.copy(), time.copy(), offset.copy(), timeZoneId)
544→
545→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
546→        other is DateTimeComponentsContents && other.date == date && other.time == time &&
547→            other.offset == offset && other.timeZoneId == timeZoneId
548→
549→    override fun hashCode(): Int =
550→        date.hashCode() xor time.hashCode() xor offset.hashCode() xor (timeZoneId?.hashCode() ?: 0)
551→}
552→
553→internal val timeZoneField = GenericFieldSpec(PropertyAccessor(DateTimeComponentsContents::timeZoneId))
554→
555→internal class TimeZoneIdDirective(private val knownZones: Set<String>) :
556→    StringFieldFormatDirective<DateTimeComponentsContents>(timeZoneField, knownZones) {
557→
558→    override val builderRepresentation: String
559→        get() =
560→            "${DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents::timeZoneId.name}()"
561→
562→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean = other is TimeZoneIdDirective && other.knownZones == knownZones
563→    override fun hashCode(): Int = knownZones.hashCode()
564→}
565→
566→internal class DateTimeComponentsFormat(override val actualFormat: CachedFormatStructure<DateTimeComponentsContents>) :
567→    AbstractDateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents, DateTimeComponentsContents>() {
568→    override fun intermediateFromValue(value: DateTimeComponents): DateTimeComponentsContents = value.contents
569→
570→    override fun valueFromIntermediate(intermediate: DateTimeComponentsContents): DateTimeComponents =
571→        DateTimeComponents(intermediate)
572→
573→    override val emptyIntermediate get() = emptyDateTimeComponentsContents
574→
575→    class Builder(override val actualBuilder: AppendableFormatStructure<DateTimeComponentsContents>) :
576→        AbstractDateTimeFormatBuilder<DateTimeComponentsContents, Builder>, AbstractWithDateTimeBuilder,
577→        AbstractWithOffsetBuilder, DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTimeComponents {
578→        override fun addFormatStructureForDateTime(structure: FormatStructure<DateTimeFieldContainer>) {
579→            actualBuilder.add(structure)
580→        }
581→
582→        override fun addFormatStructureForOffset(structure: FormatStructure<UtcOffsetFieldContainer>) {
583→            actualBuilder.add(structure)
584→        }
585→
586→        override fun timeZoneId() =
587→            actualBuilder.add(BasicFormatStructure(TimeZoneIdDirective(TimeZone.availableZoneIds)))
588→
589→        @Suppress("NO_ELSE_IN_WHEN")
590→        override fun dateTimeComponents(format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>) = when (format) {
591→            is DateTimeComponentsFormat -> actualBuilder.add(format.actualFormat)
592→        }
593→
594→        override fun createEmpty(): Builder = Builder(AppendableFormatStructure())
595→    }
596→}

598→private class TwoDigitNumber(private val reference: KMutableProperty0<Int?>) {
599→    operator fun getValue(thisRef: Any?, property: KProperty<*>) = reference.getValue(thisRef, property)
600→
601→    operator fun setValue(thisRef: Any?, property: KProperty<*>, value: Int?) {
602→        require(value === null || value in 0..99) { "${property.name} must be a two-digit number, got '$value'" }
603→        reference.setValue(thisRef, property, value)
604→    }
605→}
606→
607→private val emptyDateTimeComponentsContents = DateTimeComponentsContents()

  7→package kotlinx.datetime
  8→
  9→import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
 10→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.safeMultiply
 11→import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 12→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer
 13→import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 14→import java.time.DateTimeException
 15→import java.time.format.DateTimeParseException
 16→import java.time.temporal.ChronoUnit
 17→import kotlin.time.*
 18→import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.nanoseconds
 19→import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.seconds
 20→import java.time.Instant as jtInstant
 21→import java.time.OffsetDateTime as jtOffsetDateTime
 22→import java.time.Clock as jtClock
 23→
 24→@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 25→public actual class Instant internal constructor(internal val value: jtInstant) : Comparable<Instant> {
 26→
 27→    public actual val epochSeconds: Long
 28→        get() = value.epochSecond
 29→    public actual val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
 30→        get() = value.nano
 31→
 32→    public actual fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long = try {
 33→        value.toEpochMilli()
 34→    } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
 35→        if (value.isAfter(java.time.Instant.EPOCH)) Long.MAX_VALUE else Long.MIN_VALUE
 36→    }
 37→
 38→    public actual operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant = duration.toComponents { seconds, nanoseconds ->
 39→        try {
 40→            Instant(value.plusSeconds(seconds).plusNanos(nanoseconds.toLong()))
 41→        } catch (e: java.lang.Exception) {
 42→            if (e !is ArithmeticException && e !is DateTimeException) throw e
 43→            if (duration.isPositive()) MAX else MIN
 44→        }
 45→    }
 46→
 47→    public actual operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant = plus(-duration)
 48→
 49→    public actual operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration =
 50→        (this.value.epochSecond - other.value.epochSecond).seconds + // won't overflow given the instant bounds
 51→            (this.value.nano - other.value.nano).nanoseconds
 52→
 53→    public actual override operator fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int = this.value.compareTo(other.value)
 54→
 55→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 56→            (this === other) || (other is Instant && this.value == other.value)
 57→
 58→    override fun hashCode(): Int = value.hashCode()
 59→
 60→    actual override fun toString(): String = value.toString()
 61→
 62→    public actual companion object {
 63→        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
 64→        public actual fun now(): Instant =
 65→                Instant(jtClock.systemUTC().instant())
 66→
 67→        public actual fun fromEpochMilliseconds(epochMilliseconds: Long): Instant =
 68→                Instant(jtInstant.ofEpochMilli(epochMilliseconds))
 69→
 70→        public actual fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>): Instant =
 71→            if (format === DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET) {
 72→                try {
 73→                    Instant(jtOffsetDateTime.parse(fixOffsetRepresentation(input)).toInstant())
 74→                } catch (e: DateTimeParseException) {
 75→                    throw DateTimeFormatException(e)
 76→                }
 77→            } else {
 78→                try {
 79→                    format.parse(input).toInstantUsingOffset()
 80→                } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
 81→                    throw DateTimeFormatException("Failed to parse an instant from '$input'", e)
 82→                }
 83→            }
 84→
 85→        @Deprecated("This overload is only kept for binary compatibility", level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN)
 86→        public fun parse(isoString: String): Instant = parse(input = isoString)
 87→
 88→        /** A workaround for a quirk of the JDKs older than 11 where the string representations of Instant that have an
 89→         * offset of the form "+XX" are not recognized by [jtOffsetDateTime.parse], while "+XX:XX" work fine. */
 90→        private fun fixOffsetRepresentation(isoString: CharSequence): CharSequence {
 91→            val time = isoString.indexOf('T', ignoreCase = true)
 92→            if (time == -1) return isoString // the string is malformed
 93→            val offset = isoString.indexOfLast { c -> c == '+' || c == '-' }
 94→            if (offset < time) return isoString // the offset is 'Z' and not +/- something else
 95→            val separator = isoString.indexOf(':', offset) // if there is a ':' in the offset, no changes needed
 96→            return if (separator != -1) isoString else "$isoString:00"
 97→        }
 98→

173→public actual fun Instant.periodUntil(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): DateTimePeriod {
174→    var thisZdt = this.atZone(timeZone)
175→    val otherZdt = other.atZone(timeZone)
176→
177→    val months = thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.MONTHS); thisZdt = thisZdt.plusMonths(months)
178→    val days = thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.DAYS); thisZdt = thisZdt.plusDays(days)
179→    val nanoseconds = thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.NANOS)

181→    if (months > Int.MAX_VALUE || months < Int.MIN_VALUE) {
182→        throw DateTimeArithmeticException("The number of months between $this and $other does not fit in an Int")
183→    }
184→    return buildDateTimePeriod(months.toInt(), days.toInt(), nanoseconds)

187→public actual fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long = try {
188→    val thisZdt = this.atZone(timeZone)
189→    val otherZdt = other.atZone(timeZone)
190→    when(unit) {
191→        is DateTimeUnit.TimeBased -> until(other, unit)
192→        is DateTimeUnit.DayBased -> thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.DAYS) / unit.days
193→        is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased -> thisZdt.until(otherZdt, ChronoUnit.MONTHS) / unit.months
194→    }
195→} catch (e: DateTimeException) {
196→    throw DateTimeArithmeticException(e)
197→} catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
198→    if (this.value < other.value) Long.MAX_VALUE else Long.MIN_VALUE

 29→/**
 30→ * The minimum supported epoch second.
 31→ */
 32→private const val MIN_SECOND = -31619119219200L // -1000000-01-01T00:00:00Z
 33→
 34→/**
 35→ * The maximum supported epoch second.
 36→ */
 37→private const val MAX_SECOND = 31494816403199L // +1000000-12-31T23:59:59

 39→private fun isValidInstantSecond(second: Long) = second >= MIN_SECOND && second <= MAX_SECOND

 41→@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 42→public actual class Instant internal constructor(public actual val epochSeconds: Long, public actual val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int) : Comparable<Instant> {
 43→
 44→    init {
 45→        require(isValidInstantSecond(epochSeconds)) { "Instant exceeds minimum or maximum instant" }
 46→    }

 48→    // org.threeten.bp.Instant#toEpochMilli
 49→    public actual fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long =
 50→        epochSeconds * MILLIS_PER_ONE + nanosecondsOfSecond / NANOS_PER_MILLI
 51→
 52→    // org.threeten.bp.Instant#plus(long, long)
 53→    /**
 54→     * @throws ArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow occurs
 55→     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the boundaries of Instant are overflown
 56→     */
 57→    internal fun plus(secondsToAdd: Long, nanosToAdd: Long): Instant {
 58→        if ((secondsToAdd or nanosToAdd) == 0L) {
 59→            return this
 60→        }
 61→        val newEpochSeconds: Long = safeAdd(safeAdd(epochSeconds, secondsToAdd), (nanosToAdd / NANOS_PER_ONE))
 62→        val newNanosToAdd = nanosToAdd % NANOS_PER_ONE
 63→        val nanoAdjustment = (nanosecondsOfSecond + newNanosToAdd) // safe int+NANOS_PER_ONE
 64→        return fromEpochSecondsThrowing(newEpochSeconds, nanoAdjustment)
 65→    }
 66→
 67→    public actual operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant = duration.toComponents { secondsToAdd, nanosecondsToAdd ->
 68→        try {
 69→            plus(secondsToAdd, nanosecondsToAdd.toLong())
 70→        } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {
 71→            if (duration.isPositive()) MAX else MIN
 72→        } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
 73→            if (duration.isPositive()) MAX else MIN
 74→        }
 75→    }
 76→
 77→    public actual operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant = plus(-duration)
 78→
 79→    public actual operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration =
 80→        (this.epochSeconds - other.epochSeconds).seconds + // won't overflow given the instant bounds
 81→        (this.nanosecondsOfSecond - other.nanosecondsOfSecond).nanoseconds
 82→
 83→    actual override fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int {
 84→        val s = epochSeconds.compareTo(other.epochSeconds)
 85→        if (s != 0) {
 86→            return s
 87→        }
 88→        return nanosecondsOfSecond.compareTo(other.nanosecondsOfSecond)
 89→    }
 90→
 91→    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 92→        this === other || other is Instant && this.epochSeconds == other.epochSeconds && this.nanosecondsOfSecond == other.nanosecondsOfSecond
 93→
 94→    // org.threeten.bp.Instant#hashCode
 95→    override fun hashCode(): Int =
 96→        (epochSeconds xor (epochSeconds ushr 32)).toInt() + 51 * nanosecondsOfSecond
 97→
 98→    // org.threeten.bp.format.DateTimeFormatterBuilder.InstantPrinterParser#print
 99→    actual override fun toString(): String = format(ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET_WITH_TRAILING_ZEROS)
100→
101→    public actual companion object {
102→        internal actual val MIN = Instant(MIN_SECOND, 0)
103→        internal actual val MAX = Instant(MAX_SECOND, 999_999_999)
104→
105→        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
106→        public actual fun now(): Instant = currentTime()
107→
108→        // org.threeten.bp.Instant#ofEpochMilli
109→        public actual fun fromEpochMilliseconds(epochMilliseconds: Long): Instant =
110→            if (epochMilliseconds < MIN_SECOND * MILLIS_PER_ONE) MIN
111→            else if (epochMilliseconds > MAX_SECOND * MILLIS_PER_ONE) MAX
112→            else Instant(
113→                epochMilliseconds.floorDiv(MILLIS_PER_ONE.toLong()),
114→                (epochMilliseconds.mod(MILLIS_PER_ONE.toLong()) * NANOS_PER_MILLI).toInt()
115→            )
116→
117→        /**
118→         * @throws ArithmeticException if arithmetic overflow occurs
119→         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the boundaries of Instant are overflown
120→         */
121→        private fun fromEpochSecondsThrowing(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long): Instant {
122→            val secs = safeAdd(epochSeconds, nanosecondAdjustment.floorDiv(NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong()))
123→            val nos = nanosecondAdjustment.mod(NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong()).toInt()

125→        }
126→
127→        // org.threeten.bp.Instant#ofEpochSecond(long, long)
128→        public actual fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long): Instant =
129→            try {

131→            } catch (e: ArithmeticException) {
132→                if (epochSeconds > 0) MAX else MIN
133→            } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {

135→            }
136→

138→            fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds, nanosecondAdjustment.toLong())
139→
140→        public actual fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>): Instant = try {
141→            format.parse(input).toInstantUsingOffset()

144→        }
145→
146→        @Deprecated("This overload is only kept for binary compatibility", level = DeprecationLevel.HIDDEN)
147→        public fun parse(isoString: String): Instant = parse(input = isoString)
148→
149→        public actual val DISTANT_PAST: Instant = fromEpochSeconds(DISTANT_PAST_SECONDS, 999_999_999)
150→
151→        public actual val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant = fromEpochSeconds(DISTANT_FUTURE_SECONDS, 0)
152→    }

177→public actual fun Instant.plus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant = try {

181→            .run { if (days != 0) plus(days, DateTimeUnit.DAY) else this }

210→} catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {

221→    } catch (e: IllegalArgumentException) {

225→public actual fun Instant.periodUntil(other: Instant, timeZone: TimeZone): DateTimePeriod {
226→    var thisLdt = toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone)
227→    val otherLdt = other.toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone)
228→
229→    val months = thisLdt.until(otherLdt, DateTimeUnit.MONTH).toInt() // `until` on dates never fails
230→    thisLdt = thisLdt.plus(months, DateTimeUnit.MONTH) // won't throw: thisLdt + months <= otherLdt, which is known to be valid
231→    val days = thisLdt.until(otherLdt, DateTimeUnit.DAY).toInt() // `until` on dates never fails
232→    thisLdt = thisLdt.plus(days, DateTimeUnit.DAY) // won't throw: thisLdt + days <= otherLdt
233→    val nanoseconds = thisLdt.until(otherLdt, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND) // |otherLdt - thisLdt| < 24h
234→
235→    return buildDateTimePeriod(months, days, nanoseconds)
236→}

238→public actual fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long =

241→            toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone).dateTime.until(other.toZonedDateTimeFailing(timeZone).dateTime, unit)
242→                .toLong()
243→        is DateTimeUnit.TimeBased -> {
244→            check(timeZone); other.check(timeZone)
245→            until(other, unit)
246→        }
247→    }

  8→import kotlinx.datetime.format.*

 10→import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer

 15→/**
 16→ * A moment in time.
 17→ *
 18→ * A point in time must be uniquely identified so that it is independent of a time zone.
 19→ * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time since this would happen at different times
 20→ * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think it is already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 21→ * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 22→ * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 23→ * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 24→ *
 25→ * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 26→ * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 27→ * of the day when a leap second happens.
 28→ *
 29→ * ### Obtaining the current moment
 30→ *
 31→ * The [Clock] interface is the primary way to obtain the current moment:
 32→ *
 33→ * ```
 34→ * val clock: Clock = Clock.System
 35→ * val instant = clock.now()
 36→ * ```
 37→ *
 38→ * The [Clock.System] implementation uses the platform-specific system clock to obtain the current moment.
 39→ * Note that this clock is not guaranteed to be monotonic, and it may be adjusted by the user or the system at any time,
 40→ * so it should not be used for measuring time intervals.
 41→ * For that, consider using [TimeSource.Monotonic] and [TimeMark] instead of [Clock.System] and [Instant].
 42→ *
 43→ * ### Obtaining human-readable representations
 44→ *
 45→ * #### Date and time
 46→ *
 47→ * [Instant] is essentially the number of seconds and nanoseconds since a designated moment in time,
 48→ * stored as something like `1709898983.123456789`.
 49→ * [Instant] does not contain information about the day or time, as this depends on the time zone.
 50→ * To work with this information for a specific time zone, obtain a [LocalDateTime] using [Instant.toLocalDateTime]:
 51→ *
 52→ * ```
 53→ * val instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1709898983, 123456789)
 54→ * instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")) // 2024-03-08T12:56:23.123456789
 55→ * instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.UTC) // 2024-03-08T11:56:23.123456789
 56→ * ```
 57→ *
 58→ * For values very far in the past or the future, this conversion may fail.
 59→ * The specific range of values that can be converted to [LocalDateTime] is platform-specific, but at least
 60→ * [DISTANT_PAST], [DISTANT_FUTURE], and all values between them can be converted to [LocalDateTime].
 61→ *
 62→ * #### Date or time separately
 63→ *
 64→ * To obtain a [LocalDate] or [LocalTime], first, obtain a [LocalDateTime], and then use its [LocalDateTime.date]
 65→ * and [LocalDateTime.time] properties:
 66→ *
 67→ * ```
 68→ * val instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1709898983, 123456789)
 69→ * instant.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")).date // 2024-03-08
 70→ * ```
 71→ *
 72→ * ### Arithmetic operations
 73→ *
 74→ * #### Elapsed-time-based
 75→ *
 76→ * The [plus] and [minus] operators can be used to add [Duration]s to and subtract them from an [Instant]:
 77→ *
 78→ * ```
 79→ * Clock.System.now() + 5.seconds // 5 seconds from now
 80→ * ```
 81→ *
 82→ * Durations can also be represented as multiples of some [time-based date-time unit][DateTimeUnit.TimeBased]:
 83→ *
 84→ * ```
 85→ * Clock.System.now().plus(4, DateTimeUnit.HOUR) // 4 hours from now
 86→ * ```
 87→ *
 88→ * Also, there is a [minus] operator that returns the [Duration] representing the difference between two instants:
 89→ *
 90→ * ```
 91→ * val start = Clock.System.now()
 92→ * val concertStart = LocalDateTime(2023, 1, 1, 20, 0, 0).toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
 93→ * val timeUntilConcert = concertStart - start
 94→ * ```
 95→ *
 96→ * #### Calendar-based
 97→ *
 98→ * Since [Instant] represents a point in time, it is always well-defined what the result of arithmetic operations on it
 99→ * is, including the cases when a calendar is used.
100→ * This is not the case for [LocalDateTime], where the result of arithmetic operations depends on the time zone.
101→ * See the [LocalDateTime] documentation for more details.
102→ *
103→ * Adding and subtracting calendar-based units can be done using the [plus] and [minus] operators,
104→ * requiring a [TimeZone]:
105→ *
106→ * ```
107→ * // One day from now in Berlin
108→ * Clock.System.now().plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
109→ *
110→ * // A day and two hours short from two months later in Berlin
111→ * Clock.System.now().plus(DateTimePeriod(months = 2, days = -1, hours = -2), TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
112→ * ```
113→ *
114→ * The difference between [Instant] values in terms of calendar-based units can be obtained using the [periodUntil]
115→ * method:
116→ *
117→ * ```
118→ * val start = Clock.System.now()
119→ * val concertStart = LocalDateTime(2023, 1, 1, 20, 0, 0).toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
120→ * val timeUntilConcert = start.periodUntil(concertStart, TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
121→ * // Two months, three days, four hours, and five minutes until the concert
122→ * ```
123→ *
124→ * or [Instant.until] method, as well as [Instant.daysUntil], [Instant.monthsUntil],
125→ * and [Instant.yearsUntil] extension functions:
126→ *
127→ * ```
128→ * val start = Clock.System.now()
129→ * val concertStart = LocalDateTime(2023, 1, 1, 20, 0, 0).toInstant(TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
130→ * val timeUntilConcert = start.until(concertStart, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin"))
131→ * // 63 days until the concert, rounded down
132→ * ```
133→ *
134→ * ### Platform specifics
135→ *
136→ * On the JVM, there are `Instant.toJavaInstant()` and `java.time.Instant.toKotlinInstant()`
137→ * extension functions to convert between `kotlinx.datetime` and `java.time` objects used for the same purpose.
138→ * Similarly, on the Darwin platforms, there are `Instant.toNSDate()` and `NSDate.toKotlinInstant()`
139→ * extension functions.
140→ *
141→ * ### Construction, serialization, and deserialization
142→ *
143→ * [fromEpochSeconds] can be used to construct an instant from the number of seconds since
144→ * `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
145→ * [epochSeconds] and [nanosecondsOfSecond] can be used to obtain the number of seconds and nanoseconds since the epoch.
146→ *
147→ * ```
148→ * val instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1709898983, 123456789)
149→ * instant.epochSeconds // 1709898983
150→ * instant.nanosecondsOfSecond // 123456789
151→ * ```
152→ *
153→ * [fromEpochMilliseconds] allows constructing an instant from the number of milliseconds since the epoch.
154→ * [toEpochMilliseconds] can be used to obtain the number of milliseconds since the epoch.
155→ * Note that [Instant] supports nanosecond precision, so converting to milliseconds is a lossy operation.
156→ *
157→ * ```
158→ * val instant1 = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(1709898983, 123456789)
159→ * instant1.nanosecondsOfSecond // 123456789
160→ * val milliseconds = instant1.toEpochMilliseconds() // 1709898983123
161→ * val instant2 = Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(milliseconds)
162→ * instant2.nanosecondsOfSecond // 123000000
163→ * ```
164→ *
165→ * [parse] and [toString] methods can be used to obtain an [Instant] from and convert it to a string in the
166→ * ISO 8601 extended format.
167→ *
168→ * ```
169→ * val instant = Instant.parse("2023-01-02T22:35:01+01:00")
170→ * instant.toString() // 2023-01-02T21:35:01Z
171→ * ```
172→ *
173→ * During parsing, the UTC offset is not returned separately.
174→ * If the UTC offset is important, use [DateTimeComponents] with [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] to
175→ * parse the string instead.
176→ *
177→ * [Instant.parse] and [Instant.format] also accept custom formats:
178→ *
179→ * ```
180→ * val customFormat = DateTimeComponents.Format {
181→ *   date(LocalDate.Formats.ISO)
182→ *   char(' ')
183→ *   time(LocalTime.Formats.ISO)
184→ *   char(' ')
185→ *   offset(UtcOffset.Formats.ISO)
186→ * }
187→ * val instant = Instant.parse("2023-01-02 22:35:01.14 +01:00", customFormat)
188→ * instant.format(customFormat, offset = UtcOffset(hours = 2)) // 2023-01-02 23:35:01.14 +02:00
189→ * ```
190→ *
191→ * Additionally, there are several `kotlinx-serialization` serializers for [Instant]:
192→ * - [InstantIso8601Serializer] for the ISO 8601 extended format.
193→ * - [InstantComponentSerializer] for an object with components.
194→ *
195→ * @see LocalDateTime for a user-visible representation of moments in time in an unspecified time zone.
196→ */
197→@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
198→public expect class Instant : Comparable<Instant> {
199→
200→    /**
201→     * The number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` rounded down to a [Long] number.
202→     *

204→     * is returned by [nanosecondsOfSecond] property expressed in nanoseconds.
205→     *

207→     *
208→     * @see fromEpochSeconds
209→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.epochSeconds
210→     */

212→
213→    /**

215→     *

218→     * @see fromEpochSeconds
219→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.nanosecondsOfSecond
220→     */
221→    public val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
222→
223→    /**

227→     *
228→     * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
229→     *
230→     * @see fromEpochMilliseconds
231→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.toEpochMilliseconds
232→     */
233→    public fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long
234→
235→    /**
236→     * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the specified [duration] to this instant.
237→     *
238→     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
239→     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
240→     *
241→     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
242→     *

244→     * Consider using the [plus] overload that accepts a multiple of a [DateTimeUnit] instead for calendar-based
245→     * operations instead of using [Duration].
246→     * For an explanation of why some days are not 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased].
247→     *
248→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.plusDuration
249→     */

251→
252→    /**
253→     * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the specified [duration] from this instant.
254→     *
255→     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
256→     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
257→     *
258→     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
259→     *
260→     * **Pitfall**: [Duration.Companion.days] are multiples of 24 hours and are not calendar-based.
261→     * Consider using the [minus] overload that accepts a multiple of a [DateTimeUnit] instead for calendar-based
262→     * operations instead of using [Duration].
263→     * For an explanation of why some days are not 24 hours, see [DateTimeUnit.DayBased].
264→     *
265→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.minusDuration
266→     */
267→    public operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant
268→
269→    // questionable
270→    /**
271→     * Returns the [Duration] between two instants: [other] and `this`.
272→     *
273→     * The duration returned is positive if this instant is later than the other,
274→     * and negative if this instant is earlier than the other.
275→     *
276→     * The result is never clamped, but note that for instants that are far apart,
277→     * the value returned may represent the duration between them inexactly due to the loss of precision.
278→     *
279→     * Note that sources of [Instant] values (in particular, [Clock]) are not guaranteed to be in sync with each other
280→     * or even monotonic, so the result of this operation may be negative even if the other instant was observed later
281→     * than this one, or vice versa.
282→     * For measuring time intervals, consider using [TimeSource.Monotonic].
283→     *
284→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.minusInstant
285→     */
286→    public operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration
287→
288→    /**
289→     * Compares `this` instant with the [other] instant.
290→     * Returns zero if this instant represents the same moment as the other (meaning they are equal to one another),
291→     * a negative number if this instant is earlier than the other,
292→     * and a positive number if this instant is later than the other.
293→     *
294→     * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.compareToSample
295→     */

350→         *

362→        /**

404→         * [isDistantFuture] returns true for this value and all later ones.
405→         */
406→        public val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant // +100000-01-01T00:00:00Z

435→/**
436→ * Returns an instant that is the result of adding components of [DateTimePeriod] to this instant. The components are
437→ * added in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e., from years to nanoseconds.
438→ *
439→ * - If the [DateTimePeriod] only contains time-based components, please consider adding a [Duration] instead,
440→ *   as in `Clock.System.now() + 5.hours`.
441→ *   Then, it will not be necessary to pass the [timeZone].
442→ * - If the [DateTimePeriod] only has a single non-zero component (only the months or only the days),
443→ *   please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit.DAY] or [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], like in
444→ *   `Clock.System.now().plus(5, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())`.
445→ *
446→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
447→ * [LocalDateTime].
448→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.plusPeriod
449→ */
450→public expect fun Instant.plus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
451→
452→/**
453→ * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting components of [DateTimePeriod] from this instant. The components
454→ * are subtracted in the order from the largest units to the smallest, i.e., from years to nanoseconds.
455→ *
456→ * - If the [DateTimePeriod] only contains time-based components, please consider subtracting a [Duration] instead,
457→ *   as in `Clock.System.now() - 5.hours`.
458→ *   Then, it is not necessary to pass the [timeZone].
459→ * - If the [DateTimePeriod] only has a single non-zero component (only the months or only the days),
460→ *   please consider using a multiple of [DateTimeUnit.DAY] or [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], as in
461→ *   `Clock.System.now().minus(5, DateTimeUnit.DAY, TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())`.
462→ *
463→ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value or the results of intermediate computations are too large to fit in
464→ * [LocalDateTime].
465→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.minusPeriod
466→ */
467→public fun Instant.minus(period: DateTimePeriod, timeZone: TimeZone): Instant =
468→    /* An overflow can happen for any component, but we are only worried about nanoseconds, as having an overflow in
469→    any other component means that `plus` will throw due to the minimum value of the numeric type overflowing the
470→    platform-specific limits. */
471→    if (period.totalNanoseconds != Long.MIN_VALUE) {
472→        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -totalNanoseconds) }
473→        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone)
474→    } else {
475→        val negatedPeriod = with(period) { buildDateTimePeriod(-totalMonths, -days, -(totalNanoseconds+1)) }
476→        plus(negatedPeriod, timeZone).plus(1, DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND)
477→    }

484→ * All components of the [DateTimePeriod] returned are:
485→ * - Positive or zero if this instant is earlier than the other.
486→ * - Negative or zero if this instant is later than the other.
487→ * - Exactly zero if this instant is equal to the other.

501→ * - Negative or zero if this instant is later than the other.
502→ * - Zero if this instant is equal to the other.
503→ *
504→ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
505→ *

507→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.untilAsDateTimeUnit
508→ */
509→public expect fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit, timeZone: TimeZone): Long

511→/**
512→ * Returns the whole number of the specified time [units][unit] between `this` and [other] instants.
513→ *
514→ * The value returned is:
515→ * - Positive or zero if this instant is earlier than the other.
516→ * - Negative or zero if this instant is later than the other.
517→ * - Zero if this instant is equal to the other.
518→ *
519→ * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
520→ *
521→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.untilAsTimeBasedUnit
522→ */
523→public fun Instant.until(other: Instant, unit: DateTimeUnit.TimeBased): Long =
524→    try {

526→            NANOS_PER_ONE.toLong(),
527→            (other.nanosecondsOfSecond - nanosecondsOfSecond).toLong(),
528→            unit.nanoseconds)

530→        if (this < other) Long.MAX_VALUE else Long.MIN_VALUE
531→    }

572→ * The components of [DateTimePeriod] are calculated so that adding it back to the `other` instant results in this instant.
573→ *
574→ * All components of the [DateTimePeriod] returned are:
575→ * - Negative or zero if this instant is earlier than the other.

578→ *

788→ *
789→ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] and using [DateTimeComponents.setDateTimeOffset] in
790→ * the lambda.
791→ *
792→ * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is a format very similar to the one used by [toString].
793→ * The only difference is that [Instant.toString] adds trailing zeros to the fraction-of-second component so that the
794→ * number of digits after a dot is a multiple of three.
795→ *
796→ * @sample kotlinx.datetime.test.samples.InstantSamples.formatting
797→ */
798→public fun Instant.format(format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents>, offset: UtcOffset = UtcOffset.ZERO): String {
799→    val instant = this
800→    return format.format { setDateTimeOffset(instant, offset) }

 68→            Triple("1970-01-01t00:00:00Z", 0, 0),
 69→            Triple("1970-01-01T00:00:00z", 0, 0),
 70→            Triple("1970-01-01T00:00:00.0Z", 0, 0),
 71→            Triple("1970-01-01T00:00:00.000000000Z", 0, 0),
 72→            Triple("1970-01-01T00:00:00.000000001Z", 0, 1),
 73→            Triple("1970-01-01T00:00:00.100000000Z", 0, 100000000),
 74→            Triple("1970-01-01T00:00:01Z", 1, 0),
 75→            Triple("1970-01-01T00:01:00Z", 60, 0),
 76→            Triple("1970-01-01T00:01:01Z", 61, 0),

 79→            Triple("1970-01-01T01:01:01.000000001Z", 3661, 1),
 80→            Triple("1970-01-02T01:01:01.100000000Z", 90061, 100000000))
 81→        instants.forEach {
 82→            val (str, seconds, nanos) = it
 83→            val instant = Instant.parse(str)
 84→            assertEquals(seconds.toLong() * 1000 + nanos / 1000000, instant.toEpochMilliseconds())
 85→        }

 87→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("1970-01-01T23:59:60Z")}
 88→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("1970-01-01T24:00:00Z")}

 90→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("12020-12-31T23:59:59.000000000Z") }
 91→        // this string represents an Instant that is currently larger than Instant.MAX any of the implementations:
 92→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("+1000000001-12-31T23:59:59.000000000Z") }

 96→    fun parseStringsWithOffsets() {

 98→            Pair("2020-01-01T00:01:01.02+18:00", "2019-12-31T06:01:01.020Z"),
 99→            Pair("2020-01-01T00:01:01.123456789-17:59:59", "2020-01-01T18:01:00.123456789Z"),
100→            Pair("2020-01-01T00:01:01.010203040+17:59:59", "2019-12-31T06:01:02.010203040Z"),
101→            Pair("2020-01-01T00:01:01.010203040+17:59", "2019-12-31T06:02:01.010203040Z"),
102→            Pair("2020-01-01T00:01:01+00", "2020-01-01T00:01:01Z"),
103→        )
104→        strings.forEach { (str, strInZ) ->
105→            val instant = Instant.parse(str)
106→            assertEquals(Instant.parse(strInZ), instant, str)
107→            assertEquals(strInZ, instant.toString(), str)
108→        }
109→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("2020-01-01T00:01:01+18:01") }
110→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("2020-01-01T00:01:01+1801") }
111→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("2020-01-01T00:01:01+0") }
112→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("2020-01-01T00:01:01+") }
113→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("2020-01-01T00:01:01") }
114→        assertInvalidFormat { Instant.parse("2020-01-01T00:01:01+000000") }
115→
116→        val instants = listOf(
117→            Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE,
118→            Instant.DISTANT_PAST,
119→            Instant.fromEpochSeconds(0, 0))
120→
121→        val offsetStrings = listOf(
122→            "Z",
123→            "+03:12:14",
124→            "-03:12:14",
125→            "+02:35",
126→            "-02:35",
127→            "+04",
128→            "-04",
129→        )
130→
131→        val offsetFormat = UtcOffset.Format {
132→            optional("Z") {
133→                offsetHours()
134→                optional {
135→                    char(':'); offsetMinutesOfHour()
136→                    optional { char(':'); offsetSecondsOfMinute() }
137→                }
138→            }
139→        }
140→        val offsets = offsetStrings.map { UtcOffset.parse(it, offsetFormat) }
141→
142→        for (instant in instants) {
143→            for (offsetIx in offsets.indices) {
144→                val str = instant.format(DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET, offsets[offsetIx])
145→                val offsetString = offsets[offsetIx].toString()
146→                assertEquals(offsetString, offsetString.commonSuffixWith(str))
147→                assertEquals(instant, Instant.parse(str, DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET))
148→                assertEquals(instant, Instant.parse(str))
149→            }
150→        }
151→    }

153→    @Test
154→    fun instantCalendarArithmetic() {
155→        val zone = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
156→
157→        fun expectBetween(instant1: Instant, instant2: Instant, expected: Long, unit: DateTimeUnit) {
158→            assertEquals(expected, instant1.until(instant2, unit, zone), "i1.until(i2)")
159→            assertEquals(expected, -instant2.until(instant1, unit, zone), "i2.until(i1)")
160→            assertEquals(expected, instant2.minus(instant1, unit, zone), "i2.minus(i1)")
161→            assertEquals(expected, -instant1.minus(instant2, unit, zone), "i1.minus(i2)")
162→
163→            for (timeUnit in listOf(DateTimeUnit.MICROSECOND, DateTimeUnit.MILLISECOND, DateTimeUnit.SECOND, DateTimeUnit.MINUTE, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)) {
164→                val diff = instant2.minus(instant1, timeUnit, zone)
165→                assertEquals(instant2 - instant1, timeUnit.duration * diff.toDouble())
166→                assertEquals(instant2, instant1.plus(diff, timeUnit, zone))
167→                assertEquals(instant1, instant2.minus(diff, timeUnit, zone))
168→                assertEquals(instant2, instant1.plus(diff, timeUnit))
169→                assertEquals(instant1, instant2.minus(diff, timeUnit))
170→            }
171→        }
172→
173→        val instant1 = LocalDateTime(2019, Month.OCTOBER, 27, 2, 59).toInstant(zone)
174→        checkComponents(instant1.toLocalDateTime(zone), 2019, 10, 27, 2, 59)
175→
176→        val instant2 = instant1.plus(DateTimePeriod(hours = 24), zone)
177→        checkComponents(instant2.toLocalDateTime(zone), 2019, 10, 28, 1, 59)
178→        expectBetween(instant1, instant2, 24, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)
179→        assertEquals(instant1, instant2.minus(DateTimePeriod(hours = 24), zone))
180→
181→        val instant3 = instant1.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, zone)
182→        checkComponents(instant3.toLocalDateTime(zone), 2019, 10, 28, 2, 59)
183→        expectBetween(instant1, instant3, 25, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)
184→        expectBetween(instant1, instant3, 1, DateTimeUnit.DAY)
185→        assertEquals(1, instant1.daysUntil(instant3, zone))
186→        assertEquals(instant1.minus(1, DateTimeUnit.HOUR), instant2.minus(1, DateTimeUnit.DAY, zone))
187→
188→        val instant4 = instant1.plus(14, DateTimeUnit.MONTH, zone)
189→        checkComponents(instant4.toLocalDateTime(zone), 2020, 12, 27, 2, 59)
190→        expectBetween(instant1, instant4, 1, DateTimeUnit.YEAR)
191→        expectBetween(instant1, instant4, 4, DateTimeUnit.QUARTER)
192→        expectBetween(instant1, instant4, 14, DateTimeUnit.MONTH)
193→        expectBetween(instant1, instant4, 61, DateTimeUnit.WEEK)
194→        expectBetween(instant1, instant4, 366 + 31 + 30, DateTimeUnit.DAY)
195→        expectBetween(instant1, instant4, (366 + 31 + 30) * 24 + 1, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)
196→        assertEquals(instant1.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.HOUR), instant4.minus(14, DateTimeUnit.MONTH, zone))
197→
198→        val period = DateTimePeriod(days = 1, hours = 1)
199→        val instant5 = instant1.plus(period, zone)
200→        checkComponents(instant5.toLocalDateTime(zone), 2019, 10, 28, 3, 59)
201→        assertEquals(period, instant1.periodUntil(instant5, zone))
202→        assertEquals(period, instant5.minus(instant1, zone))
203→        assertEquals(26.hours, instant5.minus(instant1))
204→        assertEquals(instant1.plus(1, DateTimeUnit.HOUR), instant5.minus(period, zone))
205→
206→        val instant6 = instant1.plus(23, DateTimeUnit.HOUR, zone)
207→        checkComponents(instant6.toLocalDateTime(zone), 2019, 10, 28, 0, 59)
208→        expectBetween(instant1, instant6, 23, DateTimeUnit.HOUR)
209→        expectBetween(instant1, instant6, 0, DateTimeUnit.DAY)
210→        assertEquals(instant1, instant6.minus(23, DateTimeUnit.HOUR, zone))
211→    }

225→    @Test
226→    fun unitMultiplesUntil() {
227→        val unit1000days = DateTimeUnit.DAY * 1000
228→        val unit4years = DateTimeUnit.YEAR * 4 // longer than 1000-DAY
229→
230→        val zone = TimeZone.UTC
231→        val min = LocalDateTime.MIN.toInstant(zone)
232→        val max = LocalDateTime.MAX.toInstant(zone)
233→        val diffDays = min.until(max, unit1000days, zone)
234→        val diffYears = min.until(max, unit4years, zone)
235→        assertTrue(diffDays in 0..Int.MAX_VALUE, "difference in $unit1000days should fit in Int, was $diffDays")
236→        assertTrue(diffDays > diffYears, "difference in $unit1000days unit must be more than in $unit4years unit, was $diffDays $diffYears")
237→
238→        val unit500ns = DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 500
239→        val start = Instant.parse("1700-01-01T00:00:00Z")
240→        val end = start.plus(300, DateTimeUnit.YEAR, zone)
241→        val diffNs = start.until(end, unit500ns, zone)
242→        val diffUs = start.until(end, DateTimeUnit.MICROSECOND, zone)
243→        assertEquals(diffUs * 2, diffNs)
244→
245→        assertEquals(end, start.plus(diffNs, unit500ns, zone))
246→        assertEquals(start, end.plus(-diffUs, DateTimeUnit.MICROSECOND, zone))
247→    }

270→        // TODO: fails on JS

337→    @Test
338→    fun nanosecondAdjustment() {
339→        for (i in -2..2L) {
340→            for (j in 0..9) {
341→                val t: Instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(i, j)
342→                val t2: Instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(i, j.toLong())
343→                assertEquals(i, t.epochSeconds)
344→                assertEquals(j, t.nanosecondsOfSecond)
345→                assertEquals(t, t2)
346→            }
347→            for (j in -10..-1) {
348→                val t: Instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(i, j)
349→                val t2: Instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(i, j.toLong())
350→                assertEquals(i - 1, t.epochSeconds)
351→                assertEquals(j + 1000000000, t.nanosecondsOfSecond)
352→                assertEquals(t, t2)
353→            }
354→            for (j in 999_999_990..999_999_999) {
355→                val t: Instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(i, j)
356→                val t2: Instant = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(i, j.toLong())
357→                assertEquals(i, t.epochSeconds)
358→                assertEquals(j, t.nanosecondsOfSecond)
359→                assertEquals(t, t2)
360→            }
361→        }
362→        val t = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(0, Int.MAX_VALUE)
363→        assertEquals((Int.MAX_VALUE / 1_000_000_000).toLong(), t.epochSeconds)
364→        assertEquals(Int.MAX_VALUE % 1_000_000_000, t.nanosecondsOfSecond)
365→        val t2 = Instant.fromEpochSeconds(0, Long.MAX_VALUE)
366→        assertEquals(Long.MAX_VALUE / 1_000_000_000, t2.epochSeconds)
367→        assertEquals((Long.MAX_VALUE % 1_000_000_000).toInt(), t2.nanosecondsOfSecond)
368→    }

425→    @Test
426→    fun distantPastAndFuture() {
427→        val distantFutureString = "+100000-01-01T00:00:00Z"
428→        val distantPastString = "-100001-12-31T23:59:59.999999999Z"
429→        assertEquals(distantFutureString, Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE.toString())
430→        assertEquals(Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE, distantFutureString.toInstant())
431→        assertEquals(distantPastString, Instant.DISTANT_PAST.toString())
432→        assertEquals(Instant.DISTANT_PAST, distantPastString.toInstant())
433→        assertTrue(Instant.DISTANT_PAST.isDistantPast)
434→        assertTrue(Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE.isDistantFuture)
435→        assertFalse(Instant.DISTANT_PAST.isDistantFuture)
436→        assertFalse(Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE.isDistantPast)
437→        assertFalse((Instant.DISTANT_PAST + 1.nanoseconds).isDistantPast)
438→        assertFalse((Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE - 1.nanoseconds).isDistantFuture)
439→        assertTrue((Instant.DISTANT_PAST - 1.nanoseconds).isDistantPast)
440→        assertTrue((Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE + 1.nanoseconds).isDistantFuture)
441→        assertTrue(Instant.MAX.isDistantFuture)
442→        assertFalse(Instant.MAX.isDistantPast)
443→        assertTrue(Instant.MIN.isDistantPast)
444→        assertFalse(Instant.MIN.isDistantFuture)
445→    }
446→
